title: King Ranch chicken
subtitle: Texan layered chicken casserole with tortillas and Ro-Tel sauce
excerpt: The classic Texan layered casserole: corn tortillas, shredded chicken, and a sauce of tinned tomatoes with green chillies, soured cream, and cream of chicken, all baked together with cheddar on top until bubbling and golden. Rich, cheesy, and unapologetically hearty, a crowd-feeding casserole that gets better as it sits.
sourceNotes: King Ranch Chicken is a staple of Texas home cooking, named after the King Ranch in South Texas, one of the largest cattle ranches in the United States. Despite the name, the dish has no documented connection to the ranch itself. It appears in Texas community cookbooks from the 1960s and 1970s and is considered one of the defining casseroles of Tex-Mex home cooking.

content.text: King Ranch Chicken is assembled in layers like a lasagne: torn corn tortillas, shredded chicken, sauce, cheese, repeated until the dish is full. The sauce is the Texan pantry version, tinned tomatoes with green chillies, combined with soured cream and cream of chicken soup as the binding base. This is not a refined preparation; it is a practical one, and the result is deeply satisfying in the specific way that only a properly sauced baked casserole can be.

Poach the chicken first, then shred it roughly into large pieces, not so finely that it disappears into the sauce. The tortillas should be torn into large pieces, not crumbled, so that they form distinct layers and give the casserole some structural interest. The top layer is entirely cheddar, which should be deep golden and bubbling when the dish is done.

items.name: Jalapeño
items.prepNote: finely chopped (or use a small tin of green chillies)

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Cook the {{onion}}, {{pepper-red}}, and {{garlic}} in a little oil in a saucepan over medium heat for 6 to 8 minutes until softened. Add the {{jalapeno}} of chopped jalapeño and {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and simmer for 5 minutes. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{soured-cream}} of soured cream. Season with salt.

Spread a thin layer of sauce over the base of the baking dish. Cover with a layer of torn {{corn-tortilla}} tortilla pieces, then half the shredded chicken, then a third of the remaining sauce, then a third of the {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar. Repeat: tortillas, chicken, sauce, cheese. Finish with a final layer of tortillas, the remaining sauce, and all the remaining cheddar. Bake, uncovered, for 35 to 40 minutes until golden and bubbling. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.fix: The sauce should be loose enough to pour freely before assembly. It will thicken as it bakes. If dry when checked at 30 minutes, cover loosely with foil for the remainder
Tear into large pieces; the tortillas should be visible as distinct layers, not crumbled into the sauce
items.cause: Over-soaked before baking, or too finely torn
items.symptom: Tortillas have dissolved into the casserole and lost all texture

items.fix: Bring refrigerated filling to room temperature before assembling, or cover with foil for the first 20 minutes of baking to protect the top
items.cause: Oven too hot, or casserole assembled with cold filling
items.symptom: Cheese on top is burnt but centre is still cold

items.fix: The jalapeños and cumin should be clearly present; taste the sauce before assembling and adjust salt and cumin

content.text: King Ranch Chicken shows up in Texan community cookbooks from the 1970s on. It has been a Texas potluck staple ever since, at church suppers and family dinners alike. The dish belongs to Tex-Mex home cooking, where Mexican ingredients (corn tortillas, chillies, cumin) meet American casserole moves (cream-based sauces, cheddar on top, a baking dish in the oven). That blend grew up along the Texas-Mexico border and is now one of the most loved regional styles in American cooking. The dish feeds a crowd and travels well, which is why it stays on Texan tables.
